name: City of Badajoz - circular along the banks of the Guadiana River
name_original: Ciudad de Badajoz — circular por la ribera del Río Guadiana
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Tierra de Badajoz traverse a landscape characterized by fertile plains, extensive vineyards, and olive groves. The region features gentle undulations and limestone hills, with the Guadiana River flowing through, creating accessible paths along its "vegas." Reservoirs like Cornalvo and Proserpina offer scenic loops, while "sierra-island" mountain ranges provide more demanding climbs. This diverse terrain caters to various cycling preferences, from flat river routes to more challenging hilly sections. Best touring cycling routes around Tierra de Badajoz The most popular touring cycling route is Guadiana Natural Trail, a 13.8 miles (22 km) trail that takes 1 hour 50 minutes to complete, following the course of the Guadiana River. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is City of Badajoz - circular along the banks of the Guadiana River, an easy 6.9 miles (11 km) path that offers views of the city and river. Local touring cyclists also love the Route from Cáceres to Badajoz - Villar del Rey Reservoir — circular through Villar del Rey, an 11.7 miles (19 km) trail leading through the Villar del Rey Reservoir area, often completed in about 1 hour 21 minutes. Touring cycling around Tierra de Badajoz is defined by the Guadiana River plains, extensive agricultural landscapes, and historical routes like the Vía de la Plata. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside paths to moderate routes with more elevation. The routes in Tierra de Badajoz are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 90 reviews. More than 2000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Tierra de Badajoz's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Tierra de Badajoz?
A: Tierra de Badajoz offers a wide selection of touring cycling routes, with over 150 options available. These routes cater to various skill levels, including 34 easy, 74 moderate, and 48 difficult trails, ensuring there's something for every cyclist.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Tierra de Badajoz?
A: The region features diverse terrain, from fertile plains with extensive vineyards and olive groves to gentle undulations and limestone hills. The Guadiana River creates accessible paths along its 'vegas,' while 'sierra-island' mountain ranges offer more demanding climbs. You'll find a mix of relatively flat riverside routes and more challenging hilly sections.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Tierra de Badajoz?
A: Yes, Tierra de Badajoz has numerous routes perfect for beginners and families. Many paths along the Guadiana River are relatively flat and easy to navigate. An excellent option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022466079/city-of-badajoz-circular-along-the-banks-of-the-guadiana-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;City of Badajoz - circular along the banks of the Guadiana River&lt;/a&gt;, which is an easy 6.9-mile (11 km) path offering scenic views without significant elevation.

Q: What historical or natural landmarks can I see while cycling in Tierra de Badajoz?
A: Tierra de Badajoz is rich in history and natural beauty. You can encounter significant Roman monuments, especially around Mérida, and medieval castles like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5326855" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alcazaba of Badajoz&lt;/a&gt;. Natural features include the Guadiana River and reservoirs such as Cornalvo and Proserpina. Other notable sights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5326901" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de Palmas&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5326872" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerta de Palmas&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any specific routes that pass by notable sights or historical areas?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1021539394/guadiana-natural-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadiana Natural Trail&lt;/a&gt; follows the course of the Guadiana River, often passing Roman structures. Routes near Badajoz, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31868973" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Real, Badajoz – Bridge over the Guadiana River loop from Badajoz&lt;/a&gt;, offer views of the city's historical bridges and the Alcazaba.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes in the region?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Tierra de Badajoz are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022466079/city-of-badajoz-circular-along-the-banks-of-the-guadiana-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;City of Badajoz - circular along the banks of the Guadiana River&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022454286/route-from-caceres-to-badajoz-villar-del-rey-reservoir-circular-through-villar-del-rey" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route from Cáceres to Badajoz - Villar del Rey Reservoir — circular through Villar del Rey&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about touring cycling in Tierra de Badajoz?
A: The komoot community highly rates touring cycling in Tierra de Badajoz, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 90 reviews. Cyclists often praise the region's diverse terrain, the picturesque agricultural landscapes of olive groves and vineyards, and the opportunity to explore ancient Roman paths and medieval castles. The well-preserved natural areas and accessible river routes are also frequently highlighted.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Tierra de Badajoz?
A: Spring and autumn are generally the most pleasant seasons for touring cycling in Tierra de Badajoz. The weather is milder, and the landscapes are vibrant. Summers can be very hot, making early mornings or late afternoons more suitable for rides. Winters are typically mild but can be damp.

Q: Are there any longer touring cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: For experienced cyclists looking for longer distances, Tierra de Badajoz offers several moderate routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1275772" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bridge over the Guadiana River – Amoreira Aqueduct loop from Badajoz&lt;/a&gt; is a 50.3 km (31.3 miles) route with over 400 meters of elevation gain, providing a more challenging experience. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10347355" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bridge over the Guadiana River – Fortaleza de Elvas loop from Badajoz&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 44 km (27.3 miles) and includes significant elevation changes.

Q: Can I find cafes or amenities along the touring cycling routes?
A: Many routes pass through or near charming towns and villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, and other amenities. Especially in areas like Badajoz or Zafra, you'll have access to places to refuel and rest. It's always a good idea to check your route in advance for specific amenity points, especially on longer or more rural sections.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of reservoirs?
A: Yes, the region features reservoirs that provide scenic cycling opportunities. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022454286/route-from-caceres-to-badajoz-villar-del-rey-reservoir-circular-through-villar-del-rey" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route from Cáceres to Badajoz - Villar del Rey Reservoir — circular through Villar del Rey&lt;/a&gt; leads through the Villar del Rey Reservoir area, offering beautiful water views and natural surroundings.
tips: From this side of the Guadiana River you can see the Alcazaba de Badajoz in all its splendor. You can see the towers, walls and how they look at the river. Above, is the Fort of San Cristóbal.

It is the fourth bridge over the Guadiana River as it passes through Badajoz. It is called Real because two years before it was the Kings of Spain who laid the first stone at the beginning of its construction.

It is perhaps the most representative monument of the city. It consists of a door facing the Puente de Palmas, flanked by two solid cylindrical towers crowned with battlements, and surrounded at the top and at the base by decorative cords.

waypoints.name: Views of the Alcazaba of Badajoz
